tracks.name: General Business
tracks.description: Core business language for everyday professional life — meetings, emails, negotiations and reports.

tracks.name: Human Resources
tracks.description: The language of people: strategy, recruitment, development, engagement, culture and compliance.

tracks.name: Trade & Finance
tracks.description: Trade finance, letters of credit, commodities, markets and the vocabulary of international business.

tracks.name: Information Technology
tracks.description: Modern IT in business: systems, data, security, projects and digital transformation.

tracks.name: Fiscal & Taxes
tracks.description: Tax, compliance and fiscal obligations in a global business context.

tracks.name: Accounting
tracks.description: Financial statements, reporting, audit and the shared language of accountants worldwide.

tracks.name: Supply Chain
tracks.description: Sourcing, planning, procurement and supplier relationships in global trade.

tracks.name: Logistics
tracks.description: Shipping, freight, warehousing, customs and the movement of goods across borders.

tracks.name: UK & England
tracks.description: Professional and cultural life in the UK: etiquette, humour, history and how business really works there.

lessons.title: HR Strategy and the Business
lessons.objective: Learn basic words to talk about HR strategy and how HR helps the business grow.
lessons.intro:
HR means Human Resources. It is the area of a company that works with people.

A good HR team helps the company to grow. When the business has a plan, HR helps to make it happen. HR finds the right people, supports them, and makes sure they are happy at work.

This is called HR strategy. It is a simple idea: HR and the business work together for the same goal.
lessons.situation: Sophie is the HR director. She is in a meeting with the CEO. They talk about the company plan for next year.
lessons.grammarDetail: Simple Present with to be. We use am / is / are to describe people and things. Example: I am an HR manager. She is the director. We are a team.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these sentences clearly:
"HR is part of the business plan."
"Our team is ready to grow."
"People are our strategy."

exercises.title: 2. Fill in the gap
exercises.content:
Complete with am, is, are:
a) HR _____ very important.
b) We _____ a strong team.
c) I _____ the new HR manager.

lessons.wrapup: HR is more than hiring. It is a partner for the business.
takeaways: A good HR team supports people and the plan.

lessons.title: The Company and Its Roles
lessons.objective: Learn the names of common roles in a company and how to describe them in simple English.
lessons.intro:
Every company has many people with different jobs. We call these jobs roles.

At the top, there is the CEO, the chief executive officer. Then we have directors, managers, team leaders, and team members. Each role has a clear job to do.

When we understand the structure, we know who to talk to. We know who makes the decisions and who does the work. This is called the organisational structure.
lessons.situation: James is explaining the company structure to a new employee on their first day.
lessons.grammarDetail: Simple Present — positive sentences. Use the verb + s for he/she/it. Example: She works in HR. He reports to the director.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these roles:
"I am a team leader."
"She is our manager."
"He reports to the director."

exercises.title: 2. Match
exercises.content:
Match the role and the job:
1) CEO — a) leads a small team
2) Manager — b) runs the company
3) Team leader — c) manages a department

lessons.wrapup: Knowing the roles helps you talk to the right person.
takeaways: The CEO is at the top of the structure.

lessons.title: Numbers That Matter: HR Metrics
lessons.objective: Learn basic numbers, percentages and the idea of HR metrics and KPIs.
lessons.intro:
HR uses numbers to understand people at work. These numbers are called metrics.

For example, how many people work in the company? How many new people start every month? How many people leave? These are all HR metrics.

A KPI is a key performance indicator. It is a number that is very important for the team. When the KPI is good, the team is doing well.
lessons.situation: Harry is showing the monthly HR report to the team. He explains the main numbers.
lessons.grammarDetail: There is / There are. Use there is for one thing and there are for more than one. Example: There is one HR director. There are ten new employees.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise with numbers:
"There are twenty people in the team."
"Our turnover is five percent."
"The headcount is one hundred and twenty."

exercises.title: 2. Choose
exercises.content:
Choose is or are:
a) There _____ three new hires this month.
b) There _____ one big KPI for our team.
c) There _____ many metrics in the report.

lessons.wrapup: Numbers help HR tell the story of the people.
takeaways: A metric is a number we measure.
A KPI is a very important number.
There are many metrics in HR.

lessons.title: Performance Reviews Made Simple
lessons.objective: Learn how to talk about performance and ask simple questions in a review.
lessons.intro:
A performance review is a meeting. The manager and the employee sit together and talk about work.

They talk about the good things and the things that can improve. It is a moment to say "Well done" and also a moment to plan for the future.

A good review is calm, clear and honest. Both people listen and both people speak.
lessons.situation: Sophie is in a review meeting with Lucas, a team member. She gives feedback about the last three months.
lessons.grammarDetail: Simple Present questions. Start with Do / Does. Example: Do you like your job? Does she work in HR?

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these review phrases:
"Well done, that was great work."
"How do you feel about this project?"
"What can we improve next month?"

exercises.title: 2. Make questions
exercises.content:
Make a question with do or does:
a) _____ you like the feedback?
b) _____ she work on this goal?
c) _____ they need more training?

lessons.wrapup: A good review is a real conversation, not a test.
takeaways: A review is a meeting about performance.
We talk about strengths and weaknesses.

lessons.title: Recruitment: Finding the Right People
lessons.objective: Learn basic words for recruitment and how to describe what is happening now.
lessons.intro:
Recruitment is the process of finding new people to join the company.

First, HR writes a job description. Then, they post the job online. People send their CVs. The best candidates come for an interview. Finally, one person gets the offer and becomes a new employee.

It is a journey, step by step. The goal is simple: find the right person for the right role.
lessons.situation: Oliver is a recruiter. Today he is reading CVs and preparing interviews for a new finance role.
lessons.grammarDetail: Present Continuous. Use am / is / are + verb-ing for now. Example: I am reading CVs. She is interviewing a candidate.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these recruitment phrases:
"We are looking for a finance analyst."
"She is interviewing candidates today."
"They are preparing the offer."

exercises.title: 2. Complete
exercises.content:
Complete with the Present Continuous:
a) I _____ (read) the job description.
b) He _____ (wait) for the interview.
c) We _____ (hire) two new people.

lessons.wrapup: Recruitment is about people, not just papers.
takeaways: HR finds the right person for each role.

lessons.title: The Interview: Simple Questions
lessons.objective: Learn the most common interview questions and how to answer them in simple English.
lessons.intro:
An interview is a conversation between the recruiter and the candidate.

The recruiter asks questions to know the person. The candidate talks about their experience, their skills, and why they want the job.

Good interviews are calm and clear. You do not need difficult words — you just need to be honest and friendly.
lessons.situation: Oliver is interviewing Ana. He asks about her job, her skills and why she wants to work at Czarnikow.
lessons.grammarDetail: Wh- questions. Start with What, Where, When, Why, Who, How. Example: What is your name? Why do you want this job? How long have you worked there?

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these interview questions:
"What is your current role?"
"Why do you want to join us?"
"How do you work in a team?"

exercises.title: 2. Match
exercises.content:
Match the question and the answer:
1) What do you do? — a) Since 2022.
2) Where do you work? — b) I am an analyst.
3) How long? — c) At a bank in São Paulo.

lessons.wrapup: An interview is a chat, not an exam.
takeaways: Wh- questions help us know the candidate.

lessons.title: Employer Branding: A Great Place to Work
lessons.objective: Learn simple words to describe what makes a company a good place to work.
lessons.intro:
Employer branding is the image of the company as a workplace.

When people say "That is a great place to work", that is employer branding. It is how the company looks to candidates and to the market.

A strong employer brand helps to attract good people. Good people want to work in companies that are friendly, fair, and modern.
lessons.situation: Emily is preparing social media posts to show why Czarnikow is a great place to work.
lessons.grammarDetail: Adjectives. Adjectives describe nouns. Example: a friendly team, a fair manager, a modern office.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these company phrases:
"We are a friendly and fair team."
"Our workplace is modern and open."
"Czarnikow is a great place to grow."

exercises.title: 2. Choose the adjective
exercises.content:
Choose the best word:
a) Our team is very (friendly / office).
b) We have a (fair / fairly) system of reviews.
c) The workplace is (modern / to modernise).

lessons.wrapup: The best brand is the truth about the company.
takeaways: Employer branding is the image of the workplace.
A friendly team is a strong brand.

lessons.title: Onboarding: The First Days
lessons.objective: Learn the language of welcoming new people on their first day and first week.
lessons.intro:
Onboarding is the process of welcoming a new employee.

On the first day, HR says "Welcome", shows the office, and presents the team. The new person learns where things are and how things work.

A good onboarding is friendly and organised. When people feel welcome, they start their job with confidence.
lessons.situation: Emily welcomes a new colleague, Bruno, on his first day. She shows the office and introduces the team.
lessons.grammarDetail: Imperatives. Use the base verb for simple instructions. Example: Come with me. Sit here. Take this badge.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these welcome phrases:
"Welcome to Czarnikow, Bruno."
"Come with me, I will show you your desk."
"This is your team — please say hello."

exercises.title: 2. Order
exercises.content:
Put the first-day steps in order:
a) Meet the team
b) Sign papers at HR
c) Get the badge
d) Lunch with the manager

lessons.wrapup: A warm welcome is the best start for a new job.
takeaways: Welcome new people with a smile.
The first day is very important.

lessons.title: Training and Learning at Work
lessons.objective: Learn how to talk about training, skills, and what people can or cannot do.
lessons.intro:
Training is how we learn new things at work.

A company can offer many types of training: language classes, technical courses, or soft skills workshops. The goal is to help people do their job better.

When employees learn, the company grows with them. Training is an investment, not a cost.
lessons.situation: Harry is organising an English course for the finance team. He sends an email with the schedule.
lessons.grammarDetail: Can and cannot. Use can to talk about ability. Example: I can speak English. She cannot use Excel yet.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these training phrases:
"I can use Excel but I cannot write reports in English."
"The course starts on Monday."
"We offer free training every month."

exercises.title: 2. Write
exercises.content:
Write two sentences about you:
a) One thing you can do at work.
b) One thing you cannot do yet — and want to learn.

lessons.wrapup: Learning is the best investment for a career.
takeaways: Training helps us do our job better.
I can learn new skills every day.

lessons.title: Leaders and Leadership
lessons.objective: Learn simple words to describe a good leader and give simple advice.
lessons.intro:
A leader is a person who guides a team.

A good leader listens, explains, and helps. A leader does not need to shout. A real leader brings calm and direction.

Leadership is not only for the boss. Every person can be a leader in their own work — when they help, when they plan, when they teach others.
lessons.situation: James is in a leadership workshop. He talks about the difference between a boss and a leader.
lessons.grammarDetail: Should. Use should to give advice. Example: A leader should listen. You should take notes in the meeting.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these leadership phrases:
"A leader should listen before speaking."
"You should lead by example."
"Respect is the first step."

exercises.title: 2. Complete
exercises.content:
Complete with should:
a) A manager _____ explain the goal clearly.
b) We _____ listen to the team.
c) You _____ be calm in difficult moments.

lessons.wrapup: Leadership is about people, not about power.
takeaways: A good leader listens and guides.
A leader should lead by example.

lessons.title: Career Paths and Growing at Work
lessons.objective: Learn how to talk about careers, promotions and future plans at work.
lessons.intro:
A career is the path of your working life.

At the beginning, you start in a junior role. After a few years, you can become a senior. Later, maybe a manager, or a specialist in your area.

HR helps people to plan their career. It is called career pathing. When you know where you want to go, it is easier to arrive.
lessons.situation: Sophie is meeting Mariana to talk about her next career step in the company.
lessons.grammarDetail: Going to — future plans. Use am / is / are + going to + verb. Example: I am going to apply for a promotion. She is going to lead a new project.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these career phrases:
"I am going to apply for a senior role."
"She is going to lead the new team."
"We are going to support your plan."

exercises.title: 2. Your career
exercises.content:
Complete about you:
a) Next year, I am going to _____.
b) In five years, I am going to _____.
c) To grow, I am going to learn _____.

lessons.wrapup: A career is a long walk — step by step.
takeaways: A career path has clear steps.
I am going to grow in my role.
HR helps people plan the future.

lessons.title: Coaching and Good Feedback
lessons.objective: Learn how to give and receive simple, positive feedback at work.
lessons.intro:
Feedback is when we tell someone how they are doing.

Good feedback is clear, kind and honest. We say the good things — and also the things that can improve. We speak softly and we listen carefully.

Coaching is a type of feedback. A coach does not give answers; a coach asks questions that help the other person to think.
lessons.situation: Emily is coaching João. She asks questions to help him find his own answer.
lessons.grammarDetail: Adverbs of manner. They describe how we do something. Example: Speak clearly. Listen carefully. Work kindly.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these feedback phrases:
"You did this very well."
"Next time, please speak slowly and clearly."
"What do you think you can improve?"

exercises.title: 2. Match
exercises.content:
Match verb and adverb:
1) speak — a) carefully
2) listen — b) clearly
3) work — c) kindly

lessons.wrapup: Good feedback is a gift, not a problem.

lessons.title: Happy Teams: Engagement at Work
lessons.objective: Learn to talk about how people feel at work and what makes them happy.
lessons.intro:
Engagement is how connected people feel to their work.

When employees are engaged, they like their job, they like their team, and they feel proud of the company. They smile more. They help more.

HR wants to know how people feel. Sometimes HR sends a short survey: "How happy are you at work?" The answers help the company to improve.
lessons.situation: Emily is sending an engagement survey to the team. She explains the questions in simple words.
lessons.grammarDetail: Like + verb-ing. Example: I like working in this team. She likes helping new colleagues.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these engagement phrases:
"I like working with this team."
"We feel proud of our company."
"How happy are you at work today?"

exercises.title: 2. Write
exercises.content:
Complete about you:
a) I like _____ at work.
b) I do not like _____.
c) I feel _____ in my team.

lessons.wrapup: Happy people make great teams.
takeaways: Engagement is about how people feel.
A survey helps us know the truth.
I like working with my team.

lessons.title: Company Culture and Values
lessons.objective: Learn how to describe a company's culture and its main values in simple words.
lessons.intro:
Culture is the way things are done in a company.

Every company has its own culture: how people talk, how they dress, how they work together. Culture is also about values — the ideas that are important for everyone.

At Czarnikow, trust, respect and long-term relationships are important values. They are part of who we are.
lessons.situation: Sophie is in an onboarding meeting. She explains Czarnikow's culture and values to new employees.
lessons.grammarDetail: Have / has got. Use have got / has got to say what someone has. Example: We have got a strong culture. Czarnikow has got long traditions.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these culture phrases:
"We have got a strong culture of trust."
"Our values are respect and teamwork."
"Czarnikow has got a long tradition."

exercises.title: 2. Choose
exercises.content:
Choose have got or has got:
a) Czarnikow _____ offices in many countries.
b) We _____ a friendly team.
c) The company _____ strong values.

lessons.wrapup: Culture is how we behave when no one is watching.
takeaways: Culture is the way we work together.
Values are the important ideas of the company.
Trust and respect are key values.

lessons.title: Diversity, Equity and Inclusion
lessons.objective: Learn the basic language of diversity and inclusion in a simple and respectful way.
lessons.intro:
Diversity means people are different.

In a good company, everyone is welcome — women and men, young and older people, people from many countries and many backgrounds.

Inclusion means everyone has a place. Nobody feels alone. Everyone can speak, and everyone is heard. This is called DEI: diversity, equity and inclusion.
lessons.situation: Sophie is leading a DEI workshop. She uses simple, clear sentences to explain the ideas.
lessons.grammarDetail: Everyone / nobody. Use with singular verbs. Example: Everyone is welcome here. Nobody is left behind.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these inclusion phrases:
"Everyone is welcome in our team."
"Nobody is left behind."
"We respect every background."

exercises.title: 2. Complete
exercises.content:
Complete the sentences:
a) _____ has a voice in this meeting.
b) _____ is more important than another.
c) We believe _____ can learn from everyone.

lessons.wrapup: A diverse team is a strong team.

lessons.title: Talking to the Whole Company
lessons.objective: Learn how to describe how we communicate at work — emails, meetings and announcements.
lessons.intro:
Internal communication is how the company talks to its own people.

It can be by email, by video, by meeting or by a simple message on the intranet. Good communication is clear, short and friendly.

When people know what is happening, they feel part of the team. Good internal communication brings people together.
lessons.situation: Oliver is preparing a short video for the whole company about a new project.
lessons.grammarDetail: Prepositions of communication. Example: by email, by phone, in a meeting, on the intranet.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these comms phrases:
"I will send the news by email."
"We share updates on the intranet."
"Please join us in the meeting on Friday."

exercises.title: 2. Choose
exercises.content:
Choose the right preposition:
a) Please reply _____ email.
b) The news is _____ the intranet.
c) We will talk about it _____ the meeting.

lessons.wrapup: Clear words build strong teams.
takeaways: Good messages are clear and short.
We share news by email and in meetings.

lessons.title: Labour Laws Around the World
lessons.objective: Learn simple words to talk about rules and duties at work.
lessons.intro:
Every country has labour laws. These laws protect workers and companies.

Labour laws say how many hours you can work, how much rest you need, and how much you must be paid. Every company must follow these rules.

HR helps the company to understand and to respect these laws. It is called compliance — doing things the right way.
lessons.situation: Charlotte is explaining to new managers the main labour rules they must follow.
lessons.grammarDetail: Must / must not. Use must for obligation and must not for prohibition. Example: We must follow the law. You must not share private data.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these compliance phrases:
"We must respect the labour law."
"You must not work more than the legal hours."
"HR must check every contract."

exercises.title: 2. Choose
exercises.content:
Complete with must or must not:
a) Every employee _____ have a contract.
b) You _____ ignore the law.
c) The company _____ pay on time.

lessons.wrapup: Rules protect people — and protect the company too.
takeaways: The company must follow the rules.
Compliance means doing things the right way.

lessons.title: Salary, Benefits and the Pay Slip
lessons.objective: Learn simple words to talk about salary, benefits and basic money questions at work.
lessons.intro:
Compensation is the money you receive for your work.

It has two main parts: the salary and the benefits. The salary is the money. The benefits can be health insurance, meal vouchers, a pension plan and more.

Every month, HR sends a pay slip. The pay slip shows how much you earn and the taxes. It is important to read and understand your pay slip.
lessons.situation: Charlotte is helping Lucas to understand his first pay slip at Czarnikow.
lessons.grammarDetail: How much / how many. Use how much with money and uncountable words. Use how many with countable things. Example: How much do you earn? How many benefits do we have?

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these pay phrases:
"My salary is paid every month."
"We also have benefits like health insurance."
"How much do I earn after taxes?"

exercises.title: 2. Choose
exercises.content:
Complete with how much or how many:
a) _____ benefits do I have?
b) _____ is the meal voucher?
c) _____ tax do I pay?

lessons.wrapup: Understand your pay slip — it is your right.
takeaways: How much do I earn this month?

lessons.title: Conflicts at Work: Keep Calm
lessons.objective: Learn how to describe small conflicts at work and how to calm them.
lessons.intro:
Sometimes people do not agree. This is normal.

A small conflict can happen in any team — maybe about a project, a deadline, or a simple message. The important thing is to stay calm and to listen to the other person.

HR can help when the conflict is bigger. But often, a simple conversation is enough. Calm words solve many problems.
lessons.situation: James is helping two colleagues who had a misunderstanding about a report.
lessons.grammarDetail: Let's / Why don't we. Use these to make friendly suggestions. Example: Let's talk about it. Why don't we have a coffee and discuss?

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these calming phrases:
"Let's stay calm and listen."
"Why don't we talk about it now?"
"I understand your point."

exercises.title: 2. Transform
exercises.content:
Change into Let's:
a) We should discuss this. → _____
b) We should apologise. → _____
c) We should find a solution. → _____

lessons.wrapup: Calm words are the best tool in a conflict.
takeaways: Let's talk about it and find a solution.

lessons.title: Remote Work and Hybrid Life
lessons.objective: Learn the language of remote and hybrid work in simple, everyday English.
lessons.intro:
Many people today do not work only in the office.

Some people work from home. Others go to the office a few days a week. This is called hybrid work: part home, part office.

To work from home, we need a good laptop, a good internet connection, and a quiet place. We also need to stay in touch with the team — by email, by chat and by video calls.
lessons.situation: Harry is explaining the new hybrid work policy — three days in the office, two days at home.
lessons.grammarDetail: Prepositions of place. Example: I work from home on Mondays. I work at the office on Tuesdays. On Friday, we meet in the meeting room.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these hybrid phrases:
"I work from home on Mondays and Fridays."
"We meet at the office twice a week."
"Please join the video call at ten."

exercises.title: 2. Complete
exercises.content:
Complete with at / from / on:
a) I work _____ home on Wednesdays.
b) I meet the team _____ the office.
c) The call is _____ Friday morning.

lessons.wrapup: Hybrid work is the new normal — stay connected and organised.
takeaways: Hybrid work mixes home and office.
I work from home two days a week.

lessons.title: Aligning HR with Business Strategy
lessons.objective: Talk about how HR supports business goals, using natural business English at intermediate level.
lessons.intro:
HR has changed a lot over the last twenty years. It used to be mostly about contracts, payroll and paperwork. Today, HR is a real partner to the business.

Strategic HR means that every HR decision is connected to the company's goals. If the business is growing fast, HR focuses on recruitment. If the business wants to retain talent, HR looks at engagement and development.

A good HR leader can read the business plan and translate it into people priorities — the right hires, the right training, the right culture to make the strategy work.
lessons.situation: Sophie is presenting the HR plan for next year to the executive committee. She shows how each HR priority supports a business goal.
lessons.grammarDetail: Present Perfect vs Past Simple. Use the Present Perfect for experiences or changes connected to now: HR has become more strategic. Use the Past Simple for finished moments: We launched the new plan last March.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these executive phrases:
"Our HR strategy is fully aligned with the business plan."
"We have built a strong pipeline of internal talent."
"Last quarter, we launched the new engagement programme."

exercises.title: 2. Choose the tense
exercises.content:
Choose Present Perfect or Past Simple:
a) We (invest / have invested) heavily in leadership training this year.
b) HR (launched / has launched) the new policy last month.
c) Since 2022, we (reduced / have reduced) turnover by half.

lessons.wrapup: Strategic HR is not a support function — it's a growth function.
takeaways: Strategic HR translates business goals into people priorities.
We have moved beyond payroll — we drive the strategy.
Every HR decision should serve a business outcome.

lessons.title: Designing an Organisational Structure
lessons.objective: Describe reporting lines, team structures and organisational change at intermediate level.
lessons.intro:
An organisational structure is the framework that shows how a company is built. It defines who reports to whom, how teams work together, and where decisions are made.

Some companies use a traditional hierarchy — clear levels, from the CEO down to the entry roles. Others prefer flatter models, with fewer layers and more autonomy in each team.

There is no perfect structure. The right model depends on the size of the company, the type of business and the culture it wants to build. HR is often asked to redesign the structure when the company grows or changes direction.
lessons.situation: James is working with the leadership team on a restructuring project. Three departments will be merged into one.
lessons.grammarDetail: Passive Voice — present. Very common in business: Decisions are made at team level. A new model is being implemented this quarter. Structure: be + past participle.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these structural phrases:
"Decisions are made at regional level."
"A new reporting line is being created for the commercial team."
"Managers are expected to lead smaller, faster teams."

exercises.title: 2. Rewrite in passive
exercises.content:
Rewrite in the passive voice:
a) We review the structure every two years.
b) The CEO approves every senior hire.
c) HR manages the reorganisation.

lessons.wrapup: Structure follows strategy — never the other way around.
takeaways: A good structure matches the business model.
Decisions are made closer to the customer.
HR redesigns the structure as the company evolves.

lessons.title: Reading HR Metrics and KPIs
lessons.objective: Discuss HR data, trends and KPIs with confidence at intermediate level.
lessons.intro:
Good HR teams rely on data. Every month, they look at a dashboard with the main people metrics and decide what to do next.

Typical HR KPIs include voluntary turnover, time to hire, cost per hire, employee engagement score and internal promotion rate. Each number tells part of the story, and together they give a clearer picture of how the company treats its people.

The hardest part is not producing the numbers — it is explaining them. A good HR business partner knows how to turn a spreadsheet into a conversation with the leadership team.
lessons.situation: Harry presents the quarterly HR dashboard. Turnover is down, but time to hire is getting longer.
lessons.grammarDetail: Comparatives and superlatives. Use -er / more to compare and -est / most for the top. Example: Turnover is lower than last year. This is the highest engagement score we have ever recorded.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Say these HR data phrases:
"Turnover is slightly lower than last quarter."
"Time to hire has become significantly longer."
"This is the most engaged team in the region."

exercises.title: 2. Compare
exercises.content:
Make a comparative or superlative:
a) Q3 turnover (low) than Q2.
b) This is the (high) engagement score of the year.
c) The commercial team is (fast) to hire than finance.

lessons.wrapup: Data opens the door — the story is still told by humans.
takeaways: Every KPI tells part of the story.
Insight is more valuable than raw data.

lessons.title: Running a Fair Performance Review
lessons.objective: Lead a professional performance review and use reported speech to summarise what was said.
lessons.intro:
Performance reviews can be stressful — but they do not have to be. A good review is not a surprise. It is the natural conclusion of small conversations that happen during the year.

In the meeting, the manager shares honest feedback: what went well, what needs to improve, and what goals come next. The employee also speaks — about challenges, support needed, and career aspirations.

The real value comes later. HR often collects the main points and follows up: what was agreed, what will be measured, and when to check progress again.
lessons.situation: Sophie is writing up the notes from a performance review. She uses reported speech to summarise what the employee said.
lessons.grammarDetail: Reported speech. When we report what someone said, we usually shift the tense back one step. Example: She said she was happy with her results. He told me he would apply for the senior role next year.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these review phrases:
"I really acknowledge the quality of your work this year."
"We agreed on three development priorities for the next six months."
"Let's follow up on this in early April."

exercises.title: 2. Report it
exercises.content:
Rewrite in reported speech:
a) "I am proud of my team." → She said _____
b) "I will apply for the senior role." → He said _____
c) "We need more support from IT." → They said _____

lessons.wrapup: A real review is a promise to follow up.
takeaways: Good reviews are never a surprise.
Always agree on next steps and follow up.

lessons.title: Running a Professional Recruitment Process
lessons.objective: Describe a recruitment process step by step and give advice on best practices.
lessons.intro:
Recruitment is more than posting a job and reading CVs. It is a structured process — and every step affects the final result.

It usually starts with a clear job description. Then comes sourcing: where will we look for candidates? LinkedIn, referrals, headhunters, internal mobility. Next, the screening — a short call to check the basics. After that, one or two rounds of interviews, perhaps a technical test, and finally the offer.

A professional recruiter keeps the candidate informed at every stage. Even a rejected candidate should leave the process with respect for the company — because today they are candidates, and tomorrow they might be clients, partners or brand ambassadors.
lessons.situation: Oliver is training new recruiters. He walks them through the Czarnikow recruitment process, from briefing to offer.
lessons.grammarDetail: Modals of advice and obligation. Use should for strong advice, must / have to for obligation, and could / might for softer suggestions. Example: You should always send feedback to rejected candidates. Every hire must be approved by the business owner.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these recruiter phrases:
"You should always confirm the candidate's salary expectations early."
"Every offer must be aligned with our pay bands."
"We could consider an internal candidate before going external."

exercises.title: 2. Advise
exercises.content:
Complete with should / must / could:
a) You _____ always check references before the offer.
b) Every candidate _____ receive feedback — it is our policy.
c) We _____ reduce time to hire by improving the brief.

lessons.wrapup: Great recruitment is a great first impression of the whole company.
takeaways: Recruitment is a structured process, not a guess.
Every candidate deserves respect and feedback.
The offer is only the end of the beginning.

lessons.title: Behavioural and Competency-Based Interviews
lessons.objective: Ask and answer behavioural interview questions using the STAR method.
lessons.intro:
The most reliable way to predict future behaviour is to look at past behaviour. That is the idea behind behavioural interviews.

Instead of asking "Are you a team player?", a good interviewer asks "Tell me about a time when your team disagreed on a decision. What did you do?" The candidate cannot give a theoretical answer — they have to tell a real story.

A useful framework is STAR: Situation, Task, Action, Result. It helps candidates structure their answers and interviewers compare them fairly. Done well, behavioural interviews are the fairest way to hire, because everyone is assessed on the same competencies.
lessons.situation: Oliver is interviewing a candidate for a commercial role. He uses STAR questions to assess negotiation skills.
lessons.grammarDetail: Past Simple vs Past Continuous. STAR answers mix both: Past Simple for finished actions (I called the client) and Past Continuous for background actions (while the team was finalising the proposal).

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these STAR phrases:
"Tell me about a time when you had to manage a difficult stakeholder."
"What was the result of your decision?"
"While I was leading the project, the scope suddenly changed."

exercises.title: 2. Build a STAR answer
exercises.content:
Complete a short answer about yourself:
S — Situation: _____
T — Task: _____
A — Action: _____
R — Result: _____

lessons.wrapup: Stories beat opinions. Always ask for the example.
takeaways: STAR keeps interviews fair and comparable.
Always probe for the real outcome.

lessons.title: Building a Strong Employer Brand
lessons.objective: Talk about employer branding campaigns, reputation and how companies attract talent.
lessons.intro:
Employer branding has been one of the biggest shifts in HR over the last decade. In the past, companies only cared about how they looked to customers. Today, the way you look to candidates is just as important.

A strong employer brand is not a marketing trick. It is a truthful story about what it is really like to work at the company — supported by the experiences of current employees on platforms like Glassdoor, LinkedIn and social media.

Companies have been investing heavily in employee stories, career pages, and transparent communication about culture and values. When reality matches the story, the brand grows. When it doesn't, candidates quickly notice.
lessons.situation: Emily is working with the marketing team on a new careers page. They want to show real stories from real employees.
lessons.grammarDetail: Present Perfect Continuous. Use for actions that started in the past and continue now, especially about trends: We have been investing in employer branding since 2022.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these branding phrases:
"We have been sharing real employee stories on LinkedIn for six months."
"Our reputation as a fair employer is attracting better candidates."
"We want authenticity, not marketing."

exercises.title: 2. Use the tense
exercises.content:
Complete with Present Perfect Continuous:
a) HR ____ (work) on the new career page for three months.
b) We ____ (try) to improve the Glassdoor score.
c) The team ____ (create) employee testimonials all year.

lessons.wrapup: Your brand is what people say about you when you are not in the room.
takeaways: Employer brand is a truthful story, not a slogan.
We have been investing in authenticity.
Candidates quickly spot a fake brand.

lessons.title: A Professional Onboarding Journey
lessons.objective: Design and talk about a structured onboarding journey from day one to month six.
lessons.intro:
Onboarding used to mean a folder with policies and a welcome coffee. Today, it is a full journey that starts before day one and continues for months.

A great onboarding plan has three phases. Pre-boarding: in the days before joining, the new hire receives the laptop, reads about the team and signs the contract. The first weeks: deep introductions, shadowing, small tasks and clear goals. The first months: regular check-ins with the manager and HR to make sure everything is on track.

The numbers speak for themselves. If you run a structured onboarding, new hires become productive faster and they are far less likely to leave in the first year.
lessons.situation: Emily is redesigning the onboarding programme. She proposes a six-month journey with clear milestones.
lessons.grammarDetail: First Conditional. Used for realistic future results: If we run a strong onboarding, new hires will be productive faster. If we skip the pre-boarding, the first day will feel cold.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these onboarding phrases:
"If we start pre-boarding a week earlier, the first day will feel much smoother."
"Every new hire is assigned a buddy during the first month."
"The six-month check-in sets clear expectations."

exercises.title: 2. Build conditionals
exercises.content:
Complete with the first conditional:
a) If we assign a buddy, new hires _____ (feel) welcome.
b) If HR runs a 30-day check-in, we _____ (catch) problems early.
c) If we skip the plan, the ramp-up _____ (be) slower.

lessons.wrapup: Onboarding is the first real promise the company keeps.
takeaways: A buddy and a clear plan speed up the ramp-up.
If we onboard well, we retain better.

lessons.title: Designing Learning and Development Programmes
lessons.objective: Talk about L&D programmes, blended learning and changes in corporate training.
lessons.intro:
Corporate training has changed completely in the last ten years. It used to mean full-day classroom sessions, where everyone learned the same content at the same pace. Today, learning is much more flexible.

Modern L&D programmes combine several formats: short online modules, live virtual workshops, coaching, peer learning and on-the-job practice. This is called blended learning — and it respects the way adults really learn.

The challenge is not content. Content is everywhere. The challenge is making sure that people actually apply what they learn in their daily work. That is where HR, managers and learning partners need to work together.
lessons.situation: Harry is launching a new learning path for analysts. It mixes e-learning, live workshops and monthly peer sessions.
lessons.grammarDetail: Used to / would. Use used to for past habits and states: Training used to be all in person. Use would only for repeated past actions: We would spend whole days in classrooms.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these L&D phrases:
"Training used to be mostly in person; today it is blended."
"We would spend three full days in a classroom — now we learn in short modules."
"The focus is on application, not just content."

exercises.title: 2. Used to
exercises.content:
Transform into used to:
a) In the past, we trained only managers.
b) Before, all workshops were face-to-face.
c) People didn't track their own learning.

lessons.wrapup: Good L&D is not about content — it is about change.
takeaways: Modern learning is blended and flexible.
Application is where real learning happens.

lessons.title: Developing Leaders Across the Company
lessons.objective: Discuss leadership development, pipelines and the qualities of modern leaders.
lessons.intro:
Every company says leadership is important, but only some actually invest in developing leaders. Leadership development is a long-term commitment — it takes years, not a single workshop.

A strong programme has several pillars: self-awareness, people skills, strategic thinking, and the ability to make decisions under pressure. Good programmes mix coaching, mentoring, real stretch projects and honest feedback from peers.

The goal is not to create clones of the CEO. It is to build a diverse pipeline of leaders who can step up when the company needs them, at every level. If you didn't build that pipeline today, you would not have the leaders you need tomorrow.
lessons.situation: James is presenting a new leadership development programme for high-potential managers across the group.
lessons.grammarDetail: Second Conditional. Use for imagined or unlikely situations: If we invested more in coaching, we would see better leaders in two years.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these leadership phrases:
"If we invested more in mentoring, we would build a far stronger leadership pipeline."
"Modern leaders must be self-aware, decisive and humble."
"We empower our managers — we do not micromanage them."

exercises.title: 2. Build conditionals
exercises.content:
Complete with Second Conditional:
a) If we _____ (have) more mentors, our leaders _____ (grow) faster.
b) If the CEO _____ (delegate) more, the team _____ (feel) more ownership.
c) If HR _____ (run) a 360 feedback, results _____ (be) much richer.

lessons.wrapup: You do not find leaders — you grow them.
takeaways: Leadership development is a long-term investment.
Great leaders are self-aware and empowering.
A strong pipeline protects the future of the company.

lessons.title: Career Pathing and Succession Planning
lessons.grammar: Future forms — will, going to, present continuous
lessons.objective: Discuss career paths, internal mobility and succession planning in clear intermediate English.
lessons.intro:
Careers are rarely a straight line. Most professionals today move sideways, across functions and sometimes across countries, before they move up.

A career path is a map of possible moves from one role to another. HR designs these paths so people can see where they can go — not just up, but also across the business. Internal mobility is good for everyone: the employee grows, the manager gains a motivated colleague, and the company keeps valuable knowledge in house.

Succession planning is the serious version of this. For every critical role, HR and the business identify one or two successors — people who, with the right development, will be ready to step in when the moment comes.
lessons.situation: Sophie is leading a talent review meeting with the business leaders. They identify successors for the top twenty roles.
lessons.grammarDetail: Future forms. Use will for predictions and decisions made now, going to for plans already decided, and present continuous for scheduled events: She is joining the finance team in May. We are going to promote two managers next quarter. She'll probably be ready in twelve months.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these succession phrases:
"She will probably be ready for a senior role in the next twelve months."
"We are going to open a lateral move between commercial and trading."
"He is starting the leadership programme next quarter."

exercises.title: 2. Choose the future
exercises.content:
Choose the best future form:
a) We (will / are going to) launch the talent review in March — it's confirmed.
b) I think Maria (will / is going to) be our next country manager.
c) She (is meeting / will meet) the CEO tomorrow morning.

lessons.wrapup: A career path is a promise. Succession planning is how you keep it.
takeaways: Every critical role deserves a successor.

lessons.title: Coaching and Feedback Conversations
lessons.objective: Run a coaching conversation and give structured feedback using common business expressions.
lessons.intro:
Coaching is not about giving advice. It is about helping someone to think for themselves and find their own answer. A coach asks good questions, listens carefully, and sits in silence when it is useful.

Feedback is different — feedback is information, shared with kindness and clarity. A simple model is SBI: Situation, Behaviour, Impact. "In yesterday's meeting, when you interrupted the client three times, the impact was that she stopped sharing her concerns."

Done together, coaching and feedback create a culture where people are not afraid to grow. They stop hiding mistakes and start asking for help — and that is when real development begins.
lessons.situation: Emily is coaching a new team leader who struggles to delegate. She listens more than she speaks.
lessons.grammarDetail: Gerunds and infinitives. Some verbs are followed by -ing (avoid, enjoy, suggest), others by to + infinitive (decide, need, want). Example: She avoids delegating. She needs to trust her team more.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these coaching phrases:
"I'd like to hear how you see this situation before I say anything."
"When you said no to that project, what was the impact on the team?"
"Let me paraphrase what I just heard, to make sure I understood."

exercises.title: 2. Gerund or infinitive?
exercises.content:
Choose the right form:
a) She needs (to trust / trusting) her team.
b) I suggest (to take / taking) a short pause before answering.
c) He decided (to delegate / delegating) the next project.

lessons.wrapup: A great coach does not give answers — they ask the right questions.
takeaways: Feedback is a gift wrapped in clarity.
Coaching unlocks the other person's thinking.
SBI turns opinions into useful information.

lessons.title: Measuring and Boosting Engagement
lessons.objective: Talk about engagement surveys, pulse checks and how to act on the results.
lessons.intro:
Engagement is not the same as happiness. Happy employees are not always productive, and highly engaged employees are not always smiling. Engagement is about commitment, energy and a real connection between the person and the work.

Most companies run annual engagement surveys with long questionnaires. The problem is that the results arrive months after the issues appeared. Today, many HR teams prefer pulse surveys — short, frequent check-ins that help them pick up early signals.

The real test is not running the survey. It is what the company does afterwards. If employees take time to share feedback and nothing changes, engagement drops even further. The leaders must follow up, explain decisions and show real actions.
lessons.situation: Emily is presenting the results of a pulse survey. Engagement is down in two teams, and she wants clear actions.
lessons.grammarDetail: Phrasal verbs. Very common in business English: pick up on, act on, follow through, drop off, reach out, speak up. Learn them in context, not in isolation.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these engagement phrases:
"We picked up on early signs of burnout in the commercial team."
"Leaders must act on the results — otherwise, engagement will drop off."
"A listening culture is built when people feel safe to speak up."

exercises.title: 2. Complete
exercises.content:
Complete with phrasal verbs:
a) We need to _____ _____ the survey feedback immediately.
b) She _____ _____ that the team was exhausted before anyone else.
c) If we promise action, we must _____ _____ .

lessons.wrapup: The best survey is the one that changes something the next day.
takeaways: Engagement is commitment, not just happiness.
Follow through matters more than asking.

lessons.title: Building and Protecting Company Culture
lessons.objective: Describe a company culture clearly and explain how behaviours and rituals shape it.
lessons.intro:
Culture is not the poster on the wall with the company values. Culture is what actually happens in the room when the poster is not there. It is the sum of the small behaviours that managers and employees accept or reject every day.

A strong culture is built slowly, through rituals, stories and decisions. Who gets promoted? Who gets fired? Which behaviours are rewarded in public? These are the real signals. Every employee reads them instantly, even if HR never writes them down.

Culture is fragile. It takes years to build and months to lose. When a company grows fast, or merges with another, culture is often the first casualty — unless leaders actively protect it.
lessons.situation: Sophie is running a culture workshop after a merger. Two companies are trying to build one shared way of working.
lessons.grammarDetail: Defining relative clauses. Used to define who or what we are talking about, with who, that, which: The behaviours that we reward become the real culture. The people who are promoted send the strongest signal.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these culture phrases:
"The behaviours that we reward in public become our real culture."
"Culture is the thing that survives when no manager is watching."
"Every decision that a leader makes sends a cultural signal."

exercises.title: 2. Add a clause
exercises.content:
Connect with who / that / which:
a) The people _____ are promoted shape the culture.
b) Decisions _____ reward quick thinking build a fast culture.
c) The values _____ we live every day matter more than the ones on the wall.

lessons.wrapup: Culture eats strategy for breakfast — and lunch.
takeaways: Every promotion is a cultural message.
Protect the culture when you grow or merge.

lessons.title: Diversity, Equity and Inclusion in Practice
lessons.objective: Discuss DEI initiatives, challenges and language to describe inclusive workplaces.
lessons.intro:
DEI stands for diversity, equity and inclusion — three connected ideas that are often confused. Diversity is the mix of people in the team. Equity is about fair access and opportunities, correcting situations where some groups have been disadvantaged. Inclusion is the daily experience — whether everyone feels they belong and can contribute.

A company can be diverse on paper but not inclusive in practice. You can hire people from many backgrounds and still have meetings where only a few voices are really heard. That is why serious DEI work goes beyond the recruitment step.

Good DEI is not a separate programme run by HR. It is a daily practice — in the way we design meetings, promote people, give feedback and use language. It belongs to every manager, not just to the diversity team.
lessons.situation: Sophie is presenting the annual DEI report to the board. She focuses on what worked, what didn't, and what comes next.
lessons.grammarDetail: Non-defining relative clauses. Add extra information with commas: Our DEI strategy, which was launched in 2023, focuses on inclusion. Our CEO, who chairs the DEI council, is personally involved.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these DEI phrases:
"Our DEI strategy, which started last year, is now part of every manager's goals."
"Unconscious bias, which affects every recruiter, can be reduced with structured interviews."
"Inclusion is how people feel every day, not a poster in reception."

exercises.title: 2. Add commas and clauses
exercises.content:
Add a non-defining clause:
a) Our CEO _____ is personally leading the programme.
b) The affinity groups _____ meet every month, help us listen better.
c) The new DEI policy _____ was approved in March.

lessons.wrapup: Diversity is a fact. Inclusion is a practice. Equity is a promise.
takeaways: Diverse without inclusive is a wasted opportunity.

lessons.title: Internal Communication That Actually Works
lessons.grammar: Linking words — however, therefore, in addition
lessons.objective: Explain internal communication strategies and write clear messages at intermediate level.
lessons.intro:
Too much information is as dangerous as too little. Employees today receive hundreds of emails, messages and notifications every week. When everything is marked urgent, nothing really is.

Good internal communication is not about shouting louder. It is about being clear, timely and human. A short video from the CEO often works better than a long email. A direct message from a line manager often works better than a company-wide announcement.

The biggest mistake is treating internal communication as a one-way channel — leaders talk, employees listen. Real communication is a conversation. It includes listening, answering questions and admitting what is not yet decided.
lessons.situation: Oliver is planning the communication around a new company-wide policy. He prepares talking points for managers and a short CEO video.
lessons.grammarDetail: Linking words. Use them to connect ideas: However (contrast), therefore (result), in addition (adding). Example: The policy is simple; however, its impact will be wide. Therefore, we need clear talking points. In addition, managers will receive training.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these comms phrases:
"We cascaded the news through managers first; therefore, everyone had a chance to ask questions."
"The message was clear; however, we still ran two town halls to answer doubts."
"In addition to the email, we recorded a short video from the CEO."

exercises.title: 2. Connect the ideas
exercises.content:
Use however / therefore / in addition:
a) The plan is simple. _____ we must communicate it carefully.
b) We sent the email. _____ , we ran a town hall.
c) The message is positive. _____ , some people still have concerns.

lessons.wrapup: If no one knows, it didn't happen.

lessons.title: Navigating Global Labour Laws
lessons.grammar: Modals of obligation — must, have to, be required to
lessons.objective: Discuss labour law differences across countries and explain compliance obligations.
lessons.intro:
A global company like Czarnikow employs people in many countries, and every country has its own labour law. The differences can be big: working hours, notice periods, overtime, holidays, parental leave, collective bargaining and how to terminate a contract are all governed locally.

HR must stay on top of these rules. A mistake in one country can create legal, financial and reputational damage in all the others. That is why most multinationals work with local legal advisors, and why HR business partners need a basic legal literacy.

Compliance is never only about avoiding fines. It is also about fairness and trust. When employees know that the company respects the law, they trust the company with the rest.
lessons.situation: Charlotte is preparing a training for line managers about the differences between UK, Brazilian and German labour law.
lessons.grammarDetail: Obligation modals. Must (strong, often written rules), have to (external obligation), be required to (formal, often legal). Example: In Germany, we are required to consult the works council. In Brazil, you have to pay the thirteenth salary.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these compliance phrases:
"In France, employers are required to consult staff representatives before major changes."
"In the UK, you must give written notice based on length of service."
"Every manager has to understand the basics — not only HR."

exercises.title: 2. Obligation
exercises.content:
Choose the best modal:
a) Every contract (must / could) be in the local language.
b) We (have to / might) respect the local notice period.
c) In Germany, companies (are required / are suggested) to consult works councils.

lessons.wrapup: Compliance is a silent form of respect.
takeaways: Every country has its own labour law.
Compliance builds trust, not only safety.

lessons.title: Compensation and Benefits Strategy
lessons.objective: Discuss salaries, benefits, pay bands and total rewards with business nuance.
lessons.intro:
Compensation is rarely only about money. Of course, salary matters — but research shows that, above a certain level, people choose employers based on the total package.

Modern compensation strategies combine fixed pay, variable pay (bonuses, commissions), long-term incentives and a range of benefits: health insurance, pension, meal vouchers, wellness programmes and flexible working. Together, these make up what HR calls the total rewards package.

The challenge is balance. Pay too little and you lose talent. Pay far too much above the market and you create internal tensions and long-term cost pressure. Good HR teams benchmark regularly and design pay bands that are fair, transparent and sustainable.
lessons.situation: Charlotte is presenting a new total rewards strategy. She compares Czarnikow's package with the local market.
lessons.grammarDetail: Comparatives with intensifiers. Use much, far, slightly, a little to make comparatives more precise. Example: Our bonus scheme is much more competitive. Base pay is slightly below market in one country.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these compensation phrases:
"Our base pay is slightly below the market, but our total rewards are far more competitive."
"We are introducing long-term incentives for senior managers this year."
"Pay equity is non-negotiable for us."

exercises.title: 2. Intensify
exercises.content:
Add much, far, slightly:
a) Our bonus is _____ higher than last year.
b) The base is _____ lower than the market.
c) The overall package is _____ more attractive than before.

lessons.wrapup: People join for the salary, but stay for the total experience.
takeaways: Total rewards is more than base pay.

lessons.title: Handling Conflicts and Difficult Conversations
lessons.grammar: Would / could for politeness and softeners
lessons.objective: Run a difficult conversation professionally, using diplomatic business English.
lessons.intro:
Conflict at work is unavoidable. What changes everything is how we handle it. A small issue that is ignored can turn into a toxic relationship. A difficult conversation that happens early and honestly can rebuild trust in thirty minutes.

A professional approach starts with preparation. What is the real issue? What is my goal? What facts can I share? What do I need to listen to? The meeting itself should be private, calm and respectful — never in front of others.

Language makes a huge difference. Instead of "You always interrupt me", say "I would like to talk about what happened in yesterday's meeting." Softer language is not weaker — it is what allows the other person to stay open instead of defensive.
lessons.situation: James is preparing a mediation between two senior managers. He writes down the opening sentences he wants to use.
lessons.grammarDetail: Softeners. Use would, could, might, I wonder if to make sentences more diplomatic. Example: I would like to share how I saw the situation. Could we agree on what to try next?

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these diplomatic phrases:
"I would like to share how I experienced the meeting yesterday."
"Could we agree on a different way of working together?"
"I wonder if we might find some common ground here."

exercises.title: 2. Soften
exercises.content:
Make it more diplomatic:
a) You are wrong about this. → _____
b) I want to talk now. → _____
c) We need to change the process. → _____

lessons.wrapup: The hardest conversations are usually the most useful ones.

lessons.title: Remote and Hybrid Work Policies
lessons.objective: Discuss remote and hybrid policies, their challenges and their impact on teams.
lessons.intro:
The pandemic changed how we think about work. In a few weeks, millions of employees went remote — and many of them never went back to the old model. Today, hybrid work is the norm for knowledge workers in most countries.

Designing a hybrid policy is harder than it sounds. How many days in the office? Fixed or flexible? Which roles can go fully remote? What about people in different time zones? And how do you keep culture and informal connections alive when the team is rarely together?

If the pandemic hadn't happened, many companies would still be debating whether remote work was possible. Now we know it is — and the real question is how to make it fair, productive and humane.
lessons.situation: Harry is leading a workshop on hybrid policies. He compares three models and asks managers to choose the best fit for their team.
lessons.grammarDetail: Third Conditional. Used for imagined past situations: If the pandemic hadn't happened, we wouldn't have adopted hybrid work so quickly. If we had kept the old policy, we would have lost talent.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these hybrid phrases:
"If we hadn't moved to hybrid, we would have lost half of the younger generation."
"In-person time is for collaboration, not just for attendance."
"People need boundaries — otherwise, they never truly disconnect."

exercises.title: 2. Third conditional
exercises.content:
Complete:
a) If we _____ (stay) fully remote, culture _____ (suffer).
b) If we _____ (go) back to five days, we _____ (lose) talent.
c) If I _____ (not move) to hybrid, I _____ (not find) balance.

lessons.wrapup: Hybrid is not a compromise. It is a redesign.
takeaways: Hybrid is the new default for knowledge work.
Define boundaries or burnout will do it for you.
In-person time is for the things that need people in a room.

lessons.title: HR as a Strategic Partner to the Business
lessons.objective: Articulate the strategic value of HR using sophisticated, board-level business English.
lessons.intro:
Strategic HR has moved well beyond the old rhetoric of "people are our most important asset." Every HR director now has to demonstrate, with evidence, how the people agenda moves commercial, operational and financial outcomes.

That demands a rare combination of skills: genuine fluency in the language of the business, the analytical discipline to isolate the impact of people interventions, and the credibility to challenge senior leaders when short-term pressures risk undermining long-term capability. Rarely has the HR function been under more scrutiny — and rarely has it mattered more.

The best HR leaders do three things relentlessly. They connect the talent agenda to concrete business outcomes. They make bold, sometimes unpopular choices about where to invest and where to withdraw. And they build coalitions with finance, strategy and operations, because no people initiative succeeds in isolation from the rest of the business.
lessons.situation: Sophie is presenting the multi-year people strategy to the main board. The CFO is openly sceptical about the ROI of leadership investments.
lessons.grammarDetail: Inversion for emphasis. Advanced written and formal spoken English. Example: Rarely has HR mattered more than today. Not only is the people agenda strategic — it is financial. Only by partnering with finance can we demonstrate real impact.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these executive phrases:
"Rarely have we faced a talent environment as complex as this one."
"Not only is this a people issue; it is, fundamentally, a commercial one."
"Only by reframing HR as a partner can we unlock its full value."

exercises.title: 2. Invert
exercises.content:
Rewrite with inversion:
a) HR has rarely been more visible at board level. → Rarely _____
b) We are not only investing in skills; we are rethinking the role of managers. → Not only _____
c) We can solve this only by aligning HR and finance. → Only by _____

lessons.wrapup: Strategic HR is not a seat at the table — it is the willingness to be held accountable for outcomes.
takeaways: Evidence is the price of credibility.
Partnering with finance is no longer optional.

lessons.title: Designing Adaptive Organisational Structures
lessons.objective: Discuss complex organisational design trade-offs using precise, senior-level business language.
lessons.intro:
Organisational design is one of the most underestimated disciplines in modern management. Most companies overhaul their structure every few years, rarely acknowledging that a new org chart alone changes very little. Structure is only one lever among several: authority, information flows, accountability, incentives and culture must all move together, or not at all.

Adaptive structures aim to balance two opposing pressures. On one side, the need for integration — alignment, scale, shared systems. On the other, the need for local responsiveness — speed, context, empowerment. Managed well, this tension is productive; managed poorly, it produces matrices where nothing gets decided.

The best structural decisions are made with a clear hypothesis about what the company must get radically better at in the next three to five years. Structure follows that hypothesis — not the other way around. Designing for yesterday's challenges is how companies end up reorganising every eighteen months, exhausting their leaders and losing institutional memory in the process.
lessons.situation: James is advising a business unit undergoing its third reorganisation in four years. He pushes the leadership to articulate what they must be radically better at.
lessons.grammarDetail: Participle clauses. Reduce subordinate clauses to sound more concise and formal: Having reviewed three previous reorganisations, the team concluded structure was never the real issue. Faced with mounting complexity, leaders must resist the temptation of yet another redesign.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these design phrases:
"Having reviewed three previous reorganisations, we concluded structure was never the real constraint."
"Faced with a matrix of shared accountabilities, decisions were quietly escalated upwards."
"Designed for yesterday's model, the structure is already eroding responsiveness."

exercises.title: 2. Reduce the clause
exercises.content:
Rewrite using a participle clause:
a) Because we had reviewed the data, we decided to keep the structure.
b) When faced with complexity, leaders retreated into the matrix.
c) As it was designed for a different era, the model failed.

lessons.wrapup: Structure is a promise about what the company must be great at next.
takeaways: Design for the next five years, not the last five.

lessons.title: People Analytics and Evidence-Based HR
lessons.objective: Interpret complex HR data with academic and commercial precision, avoiding overclaim.
lessons.intro:
People analytics has matured from novelty into a legitimate discipline. A decade ago, HR functions produced descriptive dashboards — how many employees, how many leavers, how many hires. Today, leading teams run predictive models, conduct controlled experiments and partner with data scientists to test causal hypotheses.

With that maturity comes greater responsibility. Correlation is not causation, and even the most elegant model can mislead if the underlying data is biased or incomplete. A good people analyst is, above all, intellectually honest: willing to say "the evidence suggests, but does not prove", rather than dressing up opinion in the language of science.

The strategic value lies in the questions, not the tools. The right question — "Which managers retain talent significantly above the organisational average, and what do they actually do differently?" — often produces more insight than a twenty-tab dashboard that answers none of the questions that leaders truly care about.
lessons.situation: Harry is reviewing a study that claims a new engagement programme increased productivity by 18 percent. He is cautious about the interpretation.
lessons.grammarDetail: Hedging language. Essential in rigorous analysis: the data suggests, it would appear that, we have some evidence that, on balance, it is plausible that. Avoid unqualified claims unless the evidence is overwhelming.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these analytical phrases:
"The data suggests a relationship, but we should be cautious about claiming causation."
"On balance, it appears the programme had a meaningful impact on retention."
"There is some evidence, albeit limited, that psychological safety drives this effect."

exercises.title: 2. Hedge
exercises.content:
Soften the claim:
a) The programme caused higher engagement. → _____
b) Managers A and B are the best. → _____
c) Remote work increases productivity. → _____

lessons.wrapup: The scarcest resource in people analytics is intellectual honesty.
takeaways: The question matters more than the model.
Hedge when the evidence is thin.

lessons.title: Rethinking Performance Management
lessons.objective: Critically evaluate performance management systems at executive level.
lessons.intro:
Few HR practices have been as fiercely debated in the last decade as performance management. The traditional annual review — ratings, forced distributions, individual bonuses — has been criticised as demotivating, backward-looking and statistically unreliable. Several well-known companies have dismantled theirs, only to quietly reintroduce elements a few years later.

The underlying tension is honest. On one hand, organisations need differentiation: to reward contribution, make investment decisions and hold people accountable. On the other, most employees experience formal ratings as reductive and anxiety-inducing, particularly when tied to compensation.

The most thoughtful systems today are hybrid. They separate development conversations, which are frequent and genuinely forward-looking, from compensation decisions, which are deliberate and transparent but less central to daily life. They also invest heavily in manager capability, recognising that no system, however elegant, can compensate for a manager who avoids honest conversations.
lessons.situation: Sophie is redesigning the performance management system. She must balance the board's demand for differentiation with strong employee pushback against ratings.
lessons.grammarDetail: Complex subordination. Senior business writing frequently uses long sentences with multiple dependent clauses: Although the annual review has been widely criticised — with some justification — few organisations that have abandoned it entirely have found a superior alternative.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these performance phrases:
"Although the annual review has been widely criticised, few of the alternatives have proved decisively superior."
"What most systems lack is not structure but honest, timely conversation."
"No rating system, however elegant, can compensate for a manager who avoids feedback."

exercises.title: 2. Reflect
exercises.content:
Answer in your own words:
a) What is the strongest argument against forced distribution?
b) Why do hybrid systems often work better than pure alternatives?
c) What is the single most important investment to make performance management work?

lessons.wrapup: No system survives weak management. None fails with strong management.
takeaways: Invest in manager capability, not just process.

lessons.title: Talent Acquisition as Competitive Advantage
lessons.objective: Position talent acquisition strategically and debate trade-offs with executive stakeholders.
lessons.intro:
In industries where talent is scarce and highly mobile, the quality of talent acquisition is no longer a back-office concern — it is a genuine source of competitive advantage. Companies that consistently hire better than their competitors compound that advantage over years, sometimes decades.

What separates the best from the rest is not the sophistication of their tools, but the discipline of their choices. They invest in deep understanding of the roles that matter most. They build long-term relationships with candidates they are not yet ready to hire. They refuse to compromise on the few characteristics that predict success, while remaining genuinely open on those that do not.

The weakest acquisition functions behave reactively — filling vacancies as they appear, with little strategic view. The strongest build a proactive pipeline, often bringing in talent before there is an open role. The return on that investment is not always visible in quarterly KPIs, but it shows up unmistakably in the quality of leadership five years later.
lessons.situation: Oliver is defending an increase in the talent acquisition budget. He must convince the CFO that proactive sourcing will pay off in two to three years.
lessons.grammarDetail: Nominalisation. Replace verbs with nouns to produce more formal, executive-level prose: The identification of critical roles precedes the development of targeted pipelines. Common in strategy documents.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these TA phrases:
"The identification of critical roles must precede any investment in sourcing."
"Our assessment of candidates should rest on a few robust predictors of success."
"The development of a proactive pipeline is a multi-year undertaking."

exercises.title: 2. Nominalise
exercises.content:
Rewrite using nominalisation:
a) We must identify critical roles. → The _____
b) We assessed three candidates. → The _____
c) We developed a new pipeline. → The _____

lessons.wrapup: Hiring decisions compound. Mediocre hires are the most expensive thing a company never sees on its P&L.
takeaways: Talent acquisition is a long game.

lessons.title: Mastering Executive-Level Interviewing
lessons.objective: Conduct and critique executive interviews with sophistication and diagnostic precision.
lessons.intro:
Interviewing a senior executive has remarkably little in common with interviewing a junior candidate. At the top of the organisation, almost every candidate has the technical credentials. The real challenge is diagnostic: can you, in ninety minutes, form a credible view of how this person thinks, leads and makes difficult decisions under pressure?

The best executive interviewers resist the temptation of the polished narrative. They probe repeatedly. They ask about failures as rigorously as successes. They pay close attention to the question behind the answer — the pattern of thinking, not the specific episode. If a candidate had handled their last transformation differently, what would have changed? What do they now wish they had done sooner?

The interview is also a two-way assessment. Senior candidates are interviewing the company at the same time. They watch how decisions are made in the process itself, how consistent the messages are across stakeholders, and whether the interviewers ask anything beyond surface-level questions. A weak process is one of the most visible signals a company can send.
lessons.situation: Oliver is running an executive interview for a country director role. He is probing a candidate's past transformation experience.
lessons.grammarDetail: Mixed conditionals. Combine past and present: If you had handled the restructuring differently, where would the business be today? If I were more risk-averse, I would not have taken the job I took in 2019.

exercises.title: 1. Read aloud
exercises.content:
Practise these executive probing questions:
"If you had approached that restructuring differently, where would the business be today?"
"What, in hindsight, do you now wish you had done much sooner?"
"Tell me about a decision you took under significant pressure that you are still proud of."

exercises.title: 2. Mixed conditional
exercises.content:
Complete:
a) If he _____ (listen) to his team in 2020, the company _____ (be) stronger now.
b) If I _____ (be) more patient then, I _____ (not fire) that person.
c) If we _____ (invest) earlier, we _____ (lead) the market today.

lessons.wrapup: Hire for how people think under pressure, not for how they describe themselves in calm.
takeaways: Probe past decisions, not polished narratives.
Pattern of thinking matters more than anecdote.
A weak process costs you the best candidates.

lessons.title: Employer Brand and the Battle for Reputation
lessons.objective: Analyse employer brand positioning and discuss reputation risks with nuance.
lessons.intro:
The employer brand of a modern company is shaped by countless signals the company barely controls: Glassdoor reviews, LinkedIn posts from former employees, leaked internal memos, interview experiences shared on Reddit, and, increasingly, the public positions the company takes on contested social issues.

That reality has forced a redefinition of what employer branding actually is. It cannot, realistically, be a carefully curated story delivered through owned channels. It has to be managed as reputation — an ongoing dialogue, where authenticity is the only defensible strategy. The moment the internal reality and the external story diverge, the market notices within weeks.

For HR leaders, this creates an uncomfortable but necessary alliance with communications, legal and the executive office. A bad first month experience for a new hire is no longer only an HR problem; it is a reputation risk. Conversely, a strong, honest culture is perhaps the most durable competitive moat a company can build.
lessons.situation: Emily is managing a reputational crisis after a former employee published a critical article. She coordinates with communications and legal.
